prompt: A company wants to estimate what their monthly Azure bill would look like before deploying a new set of resources. Which tool should they use?
explanation: The Pricing Calculator estimates costs for a new/planned Azure deployment. The TCO Calculator instead compares an existing on-premises setup against running the equivalent in Azure.

prompt: What is the key difference between Azure Policy and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)?

choices.text: Azure Policy governs resource configuration/compliance; RBAC governs who can perform actions on resources
Azure Policy and RBAC do exactly the same thing
RBAC governs resource configuration; Azure Policy governs user permissions
Azure Policy only applies to virtual machines

explanation: Azure Policy enforces rules about how resources are configured (e.g., 'storage accounts must use HTTPS'), while RBAC controls who is allowed to do what. They're complementary, not interchangeable.

prompt: A web app has a 99.95% SLA and depends on a database with a 99.99% SLA. What is the approximate composite SLA of the combined solution?
explanation: Composite SLA is calculated by multiplying the individual SLAs: 0.9995 × 0.9999 ≈ 0.9994, or about 99.94% — lower than either individual component's SLA.

prompt: Which two of the following statements about Azure support plans are correct? (Choose two.)

choices.text: Basic support is free and included with every subscription
Basic support includes full technical troubleshooting for application issues
Higher-tier plans like Professional Direct offer faster response times
All support plans cost the same regardless of tier

explanation: Basic support is free but limited to billing/subscription management, not technical troubleshooting. Paid tiers (Developer, Standard, Professional Direct, Premier/Unified) offer progressively faster response times and deeper support.

prompt: A service is currently in Public Preview. Which of the following is true?

choices.text: It typically does not have an SLA and isn't recommended for production workloads
It is fully production-ready with a guaranteed SLA
It is only available to a small group of invited customers
It has already reached General Availability

explanation: Preview features — whether Private or Public — typically come without an SLA and aren't recommended for production use. General Availability (GA) is the stage where a full SLA applies.

prompt: Which Azure tool provides personalized recommendations across cost, security, reliability, operational excellence, and performance based on how you're actually using your resources?
explanation: Azure Advisor analyzes your actual resource configuration and usage to generate personalized recommendations across those five categories.

prompt: What is the main difference between Azure Service Health and the Azure Status page?

choices.text: Service Health is personalized to a customer's specific resources; Azure Status is a public, global view
Azure Status is personalized; Service Health is public and global
They are the exact same tool with two different names
Service Health only covers billing issues

explanation: Service Health gives personalized alerts about issues affecting the specific services/regions a customer actually uses. Azure Status is a general, public dashboard not tailored to any customer.

prompt: A company already has servers on-premises and in another public cloud, and wants to apply consistent Azure Policy and RBAC rules across all of them from a single place. Which service enables this?
explanation: Azure Arc extends Azure's management and governance tools — including Policy and RBAC — to resources running outside Azure entirely, whether on-premises, in another cloud, or at the edge.

prompt: Which two of the following are true regarding Azure resource tags? (Choose two.)

choices.text: Tags are useful for organizing resources for cost reporting
Tags can be used to control who is allowed to access a resource
A resource can have multiple tags
Tags replace the need for resource groups

explanation: Tags are name/value labels used for organization and cost reporting, and a resource can have several. They are not an access-control mechanism (that's RBAC) and don't replace the resource group hierarchy.

prompt: Which team ensures fairness in their facial recognition AI by testing it across a wide range of skin tones and demographics to avoid biased results? Which Responsible AI principle does this reflect?
explanation: Fairness is the Responsible AI principle focused on ensuring AI systems treat all people fairly and avoid discriminatory bias against any group.

prompt: A company wants to package a pre-approved set of policies, RBAC role assignments, and resource templates so they can quickly deploy a compliant environment to new subscriptions. What should they use?
explanation: Azure Blueprints packages governance artifacts — policy assignments, RBAC role assignments, and resource templates — together so they can be deployed repeatably to new subscriptions.

prompt: The diagram below shows a two-tier application. Based on the composite SLA shown, is the overall SLA of the whole solution higher or lower than the App Service's own 99.95% SLA?
diagramSvg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 700 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;height:auto;background:#10161D;border-radius:8px"><defs>
  <marker id="arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
    <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#8996A6"/>
  </marker>
</defs>
<rect x="60" y="100" width="220" height="80" rx="8" fill="#171F29" stroke="#4FADA6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
<text x="170.0" y="134.0" text-anchor="middle" fill="#E7ECF2" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="600">Azure App Service</text>
<text x="170.0" y="152.0" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8996A6" font-family="IBM Plex Mono, monospace" font-size="11">SLA: 99.95%</text>
<rect x="420" y="100" width="220" height="80" rx="8" fill="#171F29" stroke="#8B7FD1" stroke-width="1.5"/>
<text x="530.0" y="134.0" text-anchor="middle" fill="#E7ECF2" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="600">Azure SQL Database</text>
<text x="530.0" y="152.0" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8996A6" font-family="IBM Plex Mono, monospace" font-size="11">SLA: 99.99%</text>
<line x1="280" y1="140" x2="420" y2="140" stroke="#8996A6" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/>
<text x="350.0" y="132.0" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8996A6" font-family="IBM Plex Mono, monospace" font-size="10">depends on</text>
<text x="350" y="220" text-anchor="middle" fill="#E8A33D" font-family="IBM Plex Mono, monospace" font-size="12">Composite SLA = 99.95% × 99.99% ≈ 99.94%</text>
</svg>

choices.text: Lower — chaining dependent services multiplies their SLAs together
Higher — chaining services always improves reliability
SLA cannot be calculated for multi-service solutions

explanation: Composite SLA multiplies the individual SLAs together (0.9995 × 0.9999 ≈ 99.94%), which is always lower than or equal to the lowest individual component — chaining dependent services doesn't improve reliability by itself.

choices.text: All subscriptions and resource groups underneath it
Only the specific resource group it was created in
Nothing — policies must be applied to every resource individually
Only new subscriptions created after the policy was applied

explanation: Policies and RBAC assignments cascade down the hierarchy — applying at the Management Group level automatically covers every subscription, resource group, and resource beneath it, including future ones.

prompt: A company needs its data transfer costs minimized. Which of the following is generally true about Azure network traffic billing?

choices.text: Outbound (egress) data transfer is typically billed; inbound is usually free
Inbound data transfer is typically billed; outbound is usually free
All data transfer, inbound and outbound, is always free
All data transfer, inbound and outbound, is always billed at the same rate

explanation: Data leaving Azure (egress) is typically billed, while inbound data transfer into Azure is generally free — an asymmetry worth remembering for cost planning.

prompt: Which tool would a company use to compare the cost of their current on-premises infrastructure against running the equivalent workload in Azure?
explanation: The Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Calculator is specifically for comparing an existing on-premises setup's cost against the Azure equivalent — not for estimating a brand-new deployment (that's the Pricing Calculator).

prompt: Which of the following would you use to find Microsoft's official compliance certifications (ISO, SOC, GDPR, etc.) for Azure?
explanation: The Microsoft Trust Center (along with the Service Trust Portal for detailed audit reports) is where Microsoft publishes its official compliance certifications and documentation.
